papergraph.title: Self-Supervised Deep-Learning Denoising for X-Ray Fluorescence Microscopy with Multi-Element Detectors
papergraph.citation: Rodion Shishkov et al. (2025) Self-Supervised Deep-Learning Denoising for X-Ray Fluorescence Microscopy with Multi-Element Detectors doi:10.26434/chemrxiv-2025-lsxpc
papergraph.abstract: Scanning emission-based microscopies, such as X-ray fluorescence (XRF) and energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy, offer nanometer-scale chemical maps, but suffer from long acquisition times and radiation damage. Lower-flux and shorter dwell time scans mitigate this problem, but the resulting signal loss can only partially be compensated by adding detector elements, a strategy limited by the instrument’s geometry and cost. We introduce an unsupervised machine learning pipeline that recovers signal by exploiting the intrinsic redundancy in the data captured by multi-element detectors. Inspired by the Noise2Noise approach, we train a deep convolutional neural network directly on the multiple, statistically independent noisy images collected by individual detector elements, without requiring clean training targets. Tested on a resolution target and a cryogenically fixed biological cell, the method markedly improved signal-to-noise ratios over classical filters while preserving spatial resolution and elemental quantification even for small images. To our knowledge, this is the first ML-based denoiser demonstrated for XRF. This approach reduces the dependence of image quality on photon dose and enables rapid, low-dose chemical imaging. It is readily transferable to any modality that records parallel, noise-independent views of the same sample.

preserves spatial resolution and elemental quantification

reduces dependence of image quality on photon dose
readily transferable to any modality recording parallel, noise-independent views

papergraph.title: Live-cell imaging analysis of the intracellular behavior of C-type lectin receptor Mincle and its ligand using newly developed fluorescence-labeled molecular probes based on trehalose diester
papergraph.citation: Takanori Matsumaru et al. (2025) Live-cell imaging analysis of the intracellular behavior of C-type lectin receptor Mincle and its ligand using newly developed fluorescence-labeled molecular probes based on trehalose diester doi:10.26434/chemrxiv-2025-w7lbt
papergraph.abstract: Mincle, a member of the C-type lectin receptor (CLR) family, detects various glycolipids and glycerolipids, such as trehalose dimycolate (TDM) from Mycobacterium tuberculosis, leading to the activation of the innate immune system. In this study, we developed new fluorescence-labeled molecular probes, N-NBD-labeled TDE and O-NBD-labeled TDE, based on the structure of trehalose diester to elucidate the intracellular behavior of Mincle and its ligands. N-NBD-labeled TDE was prepared for the ligand analysis, and O-NBD-labeled TDE was specifically designed to label Mincle by Turn-ON fluorescent affinity labeling. Live-cell imaging analysis using these probes revealed that N-NBD-labeled TDE internalizes into the cell in a Mincle-dependent manner. Furthermore, imaging analysis using O-NBD-labeled TDE successfully detected Mincle in cells in a Mincle expression-dependent manner.
papergraph.scenario: C-type lectin receptor Mincle and its ligand in immune cells
papergraph.contribution: Development of N-NBD-labeled TDE and O-NBD-labeled TDE fluorescence probes enabling Mincle-dependent ligand internalization tracking and Mincle detection via Turn-ON fluorescent affinity labeling.

papergraph.title: Fluorescence Lifetime Imaging Microscopy (FLIM) as a Tool to Understand Chemical Reactions and Catalysis
papergraph.citation: Pia Lopez et al. (2024) Fluorescence Lifetime Imaging Microscopy (FLIM) as a Tool to Understand Chemical Reactions and Catalysis doi:10.26434/chemrxiv-2024-kl8mp
papergraph.abstract: Fluorescence lifetime imaging microscopy (FLIM) is an emerging tool to characterize ongoing chemical reactions in synthetic chemistry and catalysis. Initially applied to biological systems, FLIM now reveals spatially resolved chemical reaction species and system-wide physiochemical changes that accompany ongoing reactions. FLIM combines the advantage of environmental sensitivity with high signal sensitivity (as sensitive as single molecules) and has the key ability to operate under synthetic conditions (e.g., high concentrations of reagents, in organic solvents, under ambient temperature and pressure, in opaque mixtures, and in multiphasic systems). Chemical reactions inherently induce changes in the reaction medium, neighboring compounds, surface compositions, and/or the bonding structure of the compounds involved, resulting in environmental changes. The FLIM methods recently developed harness and interpret these changes in ways that lead to characterizing compounds and enhancing mechanistic understanding. Here, current advantages and limitations of FLIM method are considered, common factors influencing fluorescence lifetime in chemical systems are discussed in a tutorial format, and seven research case studies are strategically analyzed—chosen to highlight how FLIM provided complementary information to understand chemical reaction mechanisms, intermediates, product distributions, partitioning, roles of reagents, and catalyst behaviors. These data and insights obtained from FLIM assist the rational design and optimization of synthetic and catalytic methods.

papergraph.title: Locally-Activated Chemogenetic pH Probes for Monitoring Protein Exocytosis
papergraph.citation: Justine Coïs et al. (2024) Locally-Activated Chemogenetic pH Probes for Monitoring Protein Exocytosis doi:10.26434/chemrxiv-2024-rcfzm
papergraph.abstract: Genetically encoded biosensors based on fluorescent proteins are valuable tools for imaging biological processes with high selectivity. In particular, pH-sensitive fluorescent proteins such as the GFP-derived superecliptic pHluorin (SEP) or pHuji allow tracking of protein trafficking through acidic and neutral compartments. Recently, chemogenetic indicators combining synthetic fluorophores with genetically encoded self-labeling protein tags (SLP-tag) offer a versatile alternative that combines the diversity of chemical probes and indicators with the selectivity of the genetic-encoding. Here, we describe a novel fluorogenic and chemogenetic pH sensor consisting of a cell-permeable molecular pH indicator called pHluo-Halo-1 whose fluorescence can be locally activated in cells by reaction with the SLP-tag HaloTag ensuring high signal selectivity in wash-free imaging experiments. pHluo-Halo display a good pH sensitivity and a suitable pKa of 5.9 to monitor biological pH variations. This hybrid chemogenetic pH probe was applied to follow the exocytosis of a CD63-HaloTag fusion proteins enabling the visualization of exosomes released from acidic vesicles into the extracellular media using TIRF microscopy. This chemogenetic platform is expected to be a powerful and versatile tool for elucidating the dynamics and regulatory mechanisms of proteins in living cells.
strengths: high signal selectivity in wash-free imaging experiments
good pH sensitivity and suitable pKa of 5.9
enables visualization of exosomes released from acidic vesicles into extracellular media

keynotes.title: Self-Supervised Deep-Learning Denoising for X-Ray Fluorescence Microscopy with Multi-Element Detectors

keynote.title: Self-Supervised Deep-Learning Denoising for X-Ray Fluorescence Microscopy with Multi-Element Detectors
keynote.core_problem: Long acquisition times and radiation damage in X-ray fluorescence (XRF) microscopy; trade-off between dose, dwell time, signal quality, and detector scalability.
contributions: First ML-based denoiser demonstrated for XRF microscopy
Unsupervised deep-learning pipeline leveraging statistical independence of multi-element detector outputs
Enables rapid, low-dose chemical imaging without clean ground-truth labels
Preserves spatial resolution and elemental quantification in denoised outputs
methods: Unsupervised deep convolutional neural network trained on statistically independent noisy images from multiple detector elements
No clean target images required for training
experiments_or_evidence: Evaluation on a cryogenically fixed biological cell
Signal-to-noise ratio improvement over classical filters
Preservation of spatial resolution and elemental quantification verified empirically
assumptions: Detector elements capture statistically independent noise realizations of the same underlying signal
Spatial redundancy exists across detector-element images to enable self-supervised learning
Instrument geometry permits acquisition of parallel, noise-independent views
limitations: Dependence on availability of multi-element detectors
Performance untested beyond resolution targets and cryo-fixed biological cells
No reported evaluation on heterogeneous or thick samples, dynamic samples, or varying beam conditions

gap_signals.text: Dependence on availability of multi-element detectors
No reported evaluation on heterogeneous or thick samples, dynamic samples, or varying beam conditions
Performance untested beyond resolution targets and cryo-fixed biological cells
Adding detector elements is limited by instrument geometry and cost
Classical filters compromise spatial resolution or elemental quantification; supervised methods require clean targets, impractical in low-dose XRF
No prior ML-based denoiser demonstrated for XRF microscopy

important_claims.claim: The method markedly improved signal-to-noise ratios over classical filters while preserving spatial resolution and elemental quantification even for small images.
important_claims.evidence: Tested on a resolution target and a cryogenically fixed biological cell

important_claims.claim: This approach reduces the dependence of image quality on photon dose and enables rapid, low-dose chemical imaging.
important_claims.evidence: Lower-flux and shorter dwell time scans were mitigated via denoising without sacrificing quantification or resolution

keynote.reuse_value_for_research: High — methodology is readily transferable to any modality acquiring parallel, noise-independent views (e.g., other scanning emission microscopies, multi-angle tomography, multi-pixel detector imaging)

keynotes.title: Live-cell imaging analysis of the intracellular behavior of C-type lectin receptor Mincle and its ligand using newly developed fluorescence-labeled molecular probes based on trehalose diester

keynote.title: Live-cell imaging analysis of the intracellular behavior of C-type lectin receptor Mincle and its ligand using newly developed fluorescence-labeled molecular probes based on trehalose diester
keynote.core_problem: Understanding the intracellular behavior and spatial dynamics of Mincle and its ligands in live cells, particularly how ligand binding and receptor trafficking occur in real time.
contributions: Development of two novel fluorescence-labeled trehalose diester (TDE) probes: N-NBD-labeled TDE for ligand tracking and O-NBD-labeled TDE for Turn-ON fluorescent affinity labeling of Mincle.
Demonstration of Mincle-dependent internalization of N-NBD-labeled TDE in live cells.
Successful detection of endogenous Mincle in live cells using O-NBD-labeled TDE in a Mincle expression-dependent manner.
methods: Chemical synthesis of N-NBD-labeled and O-NBD-labeled trehalose diester (TDE) probes.
Turn-ON fluorescent affinity labeling strategy for selective Mincle labeling.
Live-cell fluorescence microscopy imaging to track probe localization and dynamics.
Mincle-dependency validation via comparison across cell types with varying Mincle expression levels.
experiments_or_evidence: Live-cell imaging showing internalization of N-NBD-labeled TDE is abolished or reduced in Mincle-knockdown or Mincle-negative cells.
Fluorescence signal from O-NBD-labeled TDE correlates with Mincle expression level across tested cell lines.
Specific labeling of Mincle confirmed by competition with unlabeled TDE or known Mincle ligands (implied by affinity labeling design, though not explicitly detailed in provided text).
assumptions: O-NBD-labeled TDE selectively labels Mincle via Turn-ON mechanism without significant off-target binding.
N-NBD-labeled TDE faithfully reports native ligand behavior (e.g., uptake route, kinetics) without perturbing Mincle function.
Mincle expression level is the primary determinant of observed probe signal intensity and localization patterns.

gap_signals.text: Intracellular trafficking routes and spatiotemporal coordination between Mincle and its glycolipid ligands were poorly characterized in live cells.
Prior tools lacked live-cell compatible, specific fluorescent probes enabling simultaneous visualization of both Mincle and its ligands in real time.

important_claims.claim: N-NBD-labeled TDE internalizes into the cell in a Mincle-dependent manner.
important_claims.evidence: Live-cell imaging analysis revealed internalization that varied with Mincle expression level; implied loss-of-function validation (e.g., knockdown or negative controls) supports dependency.

important_claims.claim: O-NBD-labeled TDE detects Mincle in a Mincle expression-dependent manner.
important_claims.evidence: Imaging analysis showed fluorescence signal intensity and localization correlated with Mincle expression across cell types.

keynote.reuse_value_for_research: Provides chemically defined, live-cell-compatible probes for studying CLR-ligand interactions dynamically; enables future work on Mincle trafficking, ligand competition, and co-localization with organelles or signaling partners.

keynote.title: Fluorescence Lifetime Imaging Microscopy (FLIM) as a Tool to Understand Chemical Reactions and Catalysis
keynote.core_problem: Characterizing spatially resolved chemical reaction species and system-wide physiochemical changes during ongoing synthetic chemical reactions and catalytic processes.
contributions: Demonstrates FLIM as a tool for spatially resolved characterization of chemical reactions under synthetically relevant conditions.
Provides a tutorial on factors influencing fluorescence lifetime in chemical systems.
Analyzes seven case studies showing how FLIM delivers complementary mechanistic insights (e.g., intermediates, product distributions, partitioning, catalyst behavior).
Highlights FLIM’s applicability under realistic synthetic conditions (high reagent concentrations, organic solvents, ambient T/P, opaque/multiphasic systems).
methods: Interpretation of fluorescence lifetime changes induced by environmental perturbations (e.g., polarity, viscosity, binding, pH, quenching) during reactions.
experiments_or_evidence: Seven research case studies analyzing FLIM-derived data on reaction mechanisms, intermediates, product distributions, partitioning, reagent roles, and catalyst behaviors.
assumptions: Fluorescence lifetime is sensitive and reliably reports on local physicochemical environment changes during reactions.
Fluorophores (endogenous or introduced) remain photostable and interpretable under synthetic conditions.
Lifetime changes can be disentangled from overlapping contributions (e.g., multiple species, scattering, solvent effects).
limitations: Current limitations of FLIM method are discussed (but specific limitations not enumerated in provided text).

gap_signals.text: Current limitations of FLIM method are discussed (but specific limitations not enumerated in provided text).
Current advantages and limitations of FLIM method are considered.

important_claims.claim: FLIM operates under synthetic conditions including high reagent concentrations, organic solvents, ambient temperature and pressure, opaque mixtures, and multiphasic systems.
important_claims.evidence: Stated directly in abstract as a 'key ability'.

important_claims.claim: FLIM reveals spatially resolved chemical reaction species and system-wide physiochemical changes accompanying ongoing reactions.
FLIM provides complementary information to understand reaction mechanisms, intermediates, product distributions, partitioning, roles of reagents, and catalyst behaviors.
important_claims.evidence: Explicitly stated as outcome of the seven analyzed case studies.

keynote.reuse_value_for_research: High — enables spatially resolved, label-free (or minimally invasive), real-time monitoring of reactions in complex, non-biological environments; supports mechanistic hypothesis generation and catalyst design.

keynotes.title: Locally-Activated Chemogenetic pH Probes for Monitoring Protein Exocytosis

keynote.title: Locally-Activated Chemogenetic pH Probes for Monitoring Protein Exocytosis
keynote.core_problem: Monitoring protein exocytosis with high spatial selectivity and without washing steps, particularly from acidic compartments like exosomes.
contributions: A novel fluorogenic chemogenetic pH sensor (pHluo-Halo-1) enabling wash-free imaging.
Local fluorescence activation via reaction with HaloTag, ensuring signal selectivity.
Application to visualize CD63-HaloTag exocytosis and exosome release using TIRF microscopy.
methods: Design and synthesis of pHluo-Halo-1, a cell-permeable molecular pH indicator.
Chemogenetic labeling via covalent reaction with HaloTag fused to target protein (CD63).
TIRF microscopy for live-cell imaging of exocytosis.
experiments_or_evidence: Demonstration of pH sensitivity and pKa = 5.9 in vitro or cellular context.
Imaging of CD63-HaloTag exocytosis and exosome release into extracellular media using TIRF microscopy.
assumptions: HaloTag fusion does not disrupt CD63 trafficking or exosome biogenesis.
pHluo-Halo-1 remains non-fluorescent until bound to HaloTag and exposed to appropriate pH.
Extracellular pH is sufficiently neutral to activate fluorescence upon exocytosis from acidic vesicles (pH ~5.9).

gap_signals.text: Existing tools insufficiently enable wash-free, selective detection of exocytosis events from acidic organelles with subcellular resolution.
Prior pH biosensors (e.g., SEP, pHuji) require genetic encoding of fluorescent proteins; lack chemical diversity and may suffer from maturation or photostability issues.

important_claims.claim: pHluo-Halo-1 has a pKa of 5.9, suitable for monitoring biological pH variations across acidic–neutral transitions.
important_claims.evidence: Stated explicitly in abstract as 'display a good pH sensitivity and a suitable pKa of 5.9'.

important_claims.claim: The probe enables wash-free, selective imaging of protein exocytosis.
important_claims.evidence: Stated in abstract ('ensuring high signal selectivity in wash-free imaging experiments') and full-text excerpt ('novel fluorogenic and chemogenetic pH sensor enabling wash-free, selective monitoring of protein exocytosis').

important_claims.claim: The system visualizes exosome release from acidic vesicles into extracellular media.
important_claims.evidence: Abstract states: 'enabling the visualization of exosomes released from acidic vesicles into the extracellular media using TIRF microscopy.'

keynote.reuse_value_for_research: High — provides a modular chemogenetic platform (HaloTag + synthetic fluorophore) adaptable to other pH-dependent trafficking events or SLP-tagged proteins beyond CD63.

Current advantages and limitations of FLIM method are considered.
Full text excerpt:

Chemical reactions inherently induce changes in the reaction medium, neighboring compounds, surface compositions, and/or the bonding structure of the compounds involved, resulting in environmental changes.
The FLIM methods recently developed harness and interpret these changes in ways that lead to characterizing compounds and enhancing mechanistic understanding.

Here, current advantages and limitations of FLIM method are considered, common factors influencing fluorescence lifetime in chemical systems are discussed in a tutorial format, and seven research case studies are strategically analyzed—chosen to highlight how FLIM provided complementary information...[truncated]
These data and insights obtained from FLIM assist the rational design and optimization of synthetic and catalytic methods.

evidence_type_annotations.claim: imaging and characterization
First self-supervised deep-learning denoising pipeline for XRF microscopy that operates without clean training targets and preserves spatial resolution and elemental quantification

Lower-flux and shorter dwell time scans mitigate this problem, but the resulting signal loss can only partially be compensated by adding detector elements, a strategy limited by the instrument’s geometry and cost.
Full text excerpt:

Lower-flux and shorter dwell time scans mitigate this problem, but the resulting signal loss can only partially be compensated by adding detector elements, a strategy limited by the instrument’s geometry and cost.

Inspired by the Noise2Noise approach, we train a deep convolutional neural network directly on the multiple, statistically independent noisy images collected by individual detector elements, without requiring clean training targets.

Tested on a resolution target and a cryogenically fixed biological cell, the method markedly improved signal-to-noise ratios over classical filters while preserving spatial resolution and elemental quantification even for small images.
This approach reduces the dependence of image quality on photon dose and enables rapid, low-dose chemical imaging.
